_comment: Each entry is one species (or genus). Times in Ma (million years ago). 'periodId' must match an id in periods.json — it colors the bar and links the card to its period. 'image' is relative to the site root; if the file is missing, a placeholder is shown. Optional 'sizeImage' is a self-contained size-comparison picture (creature silhouette next to a human), shown in a compact box on the card; convention assets/images/species/size/<id>.png — omit it to hide the section. 'facts' is free-form: any key/value pairs you add will appear on the card. To add a species, copy a block and edit it.

species.name: Stegosaurus
species.summary: A bus-sized plant-eater with a double row of bony plates along its back and a spiked tail (the 'thagomizer') swung at predators like Allosaurus. Despite its size, its brain was about the size of a lime.

facts.Fun fact: Its plates may have flushed with blood for display or temperature control.

species.name: Brachiosaurus
species.summary: A giraffe-built giant: unlike most sauropods its front legs were longer than its hind legs, tilting its long neck upward to browse treetops over 12 m high that no other herbivore could reach.

species.name: Triceratops
species.summary: A rhino-like herbivore with two meter-long brow horns and a broad bony frill. One of the very last non-avian dinosaurs — it lived right up to the asteroid impact, sharing its floodplains with T. rex.

species.name: Tyrannosaurus rex
species.summary: The apex predator of Late Cretaceous North America, with the strongest bite of any land animal ever measured — enough to crush bone. Excellent eyesight and a surprisingly large brain made it a capable hunter, not just a scavenger.

facts.Found in: Prince Creek Formation, North Slope, Alaska, USA

species.name: LUCA
species.summary: The Last Universal Common Ancestor: not a fossil but an inference — the single-celled organism from which every living thing — bacteria, archaea, plants, animals, you — is descended. It likely lived in hot, mineral-rich water without oxygen, already running the core chemistry of life that all its descendants still share.

facts.Fun fact: We've never found LUCA, yet we can reconstruct hundreds of its genes from what all modern life still has in common.

species.name: Anomalocaris
species.summary: One of the first great predators — a metre-long swimmer that cruised the Cambrian seas on rows of flexible side-flaps, seizing prey with a pair of spiny front appendages and crushing it in a circular, pineapple-slice mouth. It was the apex hunter of a world where most animals were thumb-sized.

facts.Fun fact: Its mouth and grasping arms were each first described as separate animals before the whole creature was reassembled.

species.name: Wiwaxia
species.summary: A slug-sized seafloor grazer sheathed in overlapping scales, with two rows of upright blade-like spines warding off attack from above. It scraped microbial mats with a rasping mouth — an early experiment in the body plans that would lead to molluscs and worms.

facts.Fun fact: Its exact place on the tree of life is still debated — somewhere near the ancestry of molluscs and annelids.

species.name: Hallucigenia
species.summary: A tiny, dream-like worm that walked on seven pairs of clawed legs and bristled with defensive spines down its back. So bizarre that scientists first reconstructed it upside-down and back-to-front — the 'head' turned out to be the tail.

facts.Fun fact: Named for its 'hallucination-like' appearance, it's an early cousin of today's velvet worms.

species.name: Utahraptor
species.summary: The largest 'raptor' ever found — a half-tonne predator the weight of a polar bear, armed with a 23-centimetre sickle claw on each foot. Far bigger and heavier than the Velociraptor of films, it stalked the floodplains of Early Cretaceous Utah.

facts.Fun fact: Its killing claw was longer than your hand — the biggest of any dromaeosaur.

species.name: Pachyrhinosaurus
species.summary: A heavyset horned dinosaur that traded the usual nasal horn for a massive bony pad, or 'boss', over its snout — probably for head-to-head shoving matches. Great herds roamed the Arctic of Late Cretaceous Alaska, enduring months of winter darkness.

facts.Fun fact: It shared the polar Prince Creek Formation with the 'polar bear lizard' Nanuqsaurus.
